name: Russian Khmeimim Air Base (Hmeymim)
detail: Russia's primary air operations base in Syria, located near Latakia/Jableh. Established 2015. Hosts Su-24, Su-25, Su-34, Su-35 jets; Mi-8, Mi-24 helicopters; and S-400 air defense systems. Under permanent basing agreement (49 years + 25-year extensions) signed 2017. Post-Assad, Russia is in negotiations with transitional government over future status. Russia has reduced but not eliminated its Syria presence.

name: Russian Tartus Naval Facility
detail: Russia's only Mediterranean naval facility, in Tartus on Syria's coast. Upgraded from Soviet-era logistics point (1971) to a full naval base under 2017 agreement, hosting up to 11 warships including nuclear-capable submarines. Provides Moscow's Med fleet with maintenance and resupply. Status uncertain post-Assad — transitional government has not voided agreement but demands renegotiation.

name: US Forces — Northeast Syria (SDF Partnership)
detail: Approximately 900 US troops remain in northeast Syria as of April 2025, partnered with the SDF to prevent ISIS resurgence and guard al-Hol camp. Bases include Conoco oil field garrison, Mission Support Site Euphrates, and Rumailan airstrip near Qamishli. US forces have repelled multiple ISIS and Iranian proxy attacks. Their long-term presence is subject to ongoing US policy review.

name: US Garrison — At-Tanf (Al-Tanf)
detail: Small US garrison at the Al-Tanf crossing (Syria-Iraq-Jordan tripoint), approximately 150–200 personnel with Maghawir al-Thawra allied Syrian force. Purpose: intercept Iranian weapons smuggling through the 'land corridor' from Iraq. Creates a 55 km deconfliction zone. Post-Assad strategic value debated; transitional government wants US presence formalized through bilateral agreement.

name: Turkish Armed Forces — Northern Syria
detail: Turkey maintains approximately 5,000–10,000 troops in northern Syria supporting Syrian National Army (SNA) proxies. Controls ~4,000 sq km including Afrin, Ras al-Ayn, Tal Abyad, Azaz, and Al-Bab. Turkish operations: Euphrates Shield (2016–17), Olive Branch (2018), Peace Spring (2019), Spring Shield (2020). Turkey has 12 military observation posts/bases across the region. Maintains buffer zone against YPG/SDF.

name: Turkish Bayraktar TB2 UCAVs
detail: Turkey deployed Bayraktar TB2 armed drones in northern Syria since 2019, conducting strikes against YPG/SDF targets. TB2s proved decisive in multiple clashes, eliminating tanks, artillery, and air defense systems. Turkey shares TB2 technology with SNA factions. These drones significantly shifted the balance of power in Turkish-controlled areas and demonstrated Turkish unmanned warfare capability.

name: Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF)
detail: SDF: estimated 70,000–100,000 fighters across northeast Syria. Core: YPG (Kurdish People's Protection Units) plus women's units (YPJ); also Arab, Assyrian, Syriac, and Turkmen components. Holds 65,000+ ISIS detainees at al-Hol and Panorama camps. Equipped with US-supplied MRAPs, artillery, ATGMs. Faces pressure from Turkey, transitional government, and ISIS insurgents simultaneously.

name: Iranian IRGC / Quds Force (Residual Presence)
detail: Iran's IRGC-Quds Force maintained thousands of troops, commanders, and proxy militias in Syria 2012–2024. Key role: coordinate Hezbollah, Iraqi Hashd factions, and Afghan Fatemiyoun Brigade in defense of Assad. After Assad's fall (Dec 2024), IRGC withdrew most personnel. Residual influence through some Iraqi Shia militias near Deir ez-Zor. The 'land corridor' to Hezbollah is severely disrupted.

name: Israeli Air Force — Syria Strike Campaign
detail: Israel conducted 450+ airstrikes against Syrian military targets within 72 hours of Assad's fall (Dec 8–11, 2024), destroying approximately 70–80% of Syria's naval fleet, most air defense systems, and significant missile/munitions stockpiles. Ongoing strikes continued into 2025 targeting residual weapons caches. Israel also expanded its buffer zone in the Golan beyond the 1974 ceasefire line into Syrian territory, which the transitional government protests.

name: HTS Military Forces (Integrating into Syrian Army)
detail: HTS's military wing (officially dissolved January 2025) formed the backbone of the offensive that toppled Assad. Approximately 20,000–30,000 fighters, well-trained and disciplined by Syrian civil war standards. HTS fighters are being integrated into a new national army structure. Key units: Idlib-based infantry with drone, artillery, and armored vehicle capability — significantly enhanced during 2023–2024.
